label: no
cot_prefix: Okay, let's try to figure out the answer here. The question is about why the employer made all those criticisms about Julia Vassilyevna's work. The options are A through D. First, I need to recall the story. The employer is calculating how much he owes Julia, the governess. He starts by saying they agreed on thirty rubles a month, but Julia claims it was forty. He insists on thirty and then starts deducting various amounts for Sundays, holidays, days when the kids were sick, her having a toothache, breaking a teacup, the kid climbing a tree and tearing his jacket, the maid stealing shoes, and some money he claims he gave her. Each time he subtracts more money. Julia doesn't protest any of these deductions even though some seem questionable, like the employer claiming he gave her ten rubles when she says he didn't. In the end, he gives her eleven rubles but then gets angry because she says "merci" (thank you) and realizes he's been unfair. He then gives her the full eighty rubles he originally owed her and reflects that it's easy to crush the weak. The key part here is the employer's own explanation at the end. He says he played a "cruel lesson" to teach her and that he wanted to give her the entire eighty rubles because he realized she was too spineless to protest. So the employer's initial criticisms and deductions weren't because she actually did those things (like breaking the teacup or the kid climbing the tree) but because he wanted to test her reaction. He wanted her to stand up for herself and protest the unfair
